enrichment.short_description: Open-source PHP blogging platform with a plugin and theme ecosystem for building self-hosted blogs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Serendipity, commonly abbreviated s9y, is a free, open-source weblog engine written in PHP. The project began in 2002 (originally called jBlog before being renamed Serendipity) and is released under the BSD 3-Clause license.

It supports MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQLite database backends and uses the Smarty template engine, giving self-hosters a mature, framework-style publishing platform rather than a single fixed blog template. Extensibility comes through the Spartacus plugin and theme repository, which lets site owners add features such as comment handling, media management, and custom editors without touching core code.
The current stable release line is version 2.6, and the project remains actively developed on GitHub with a dedicated blog and support forums.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Serendipity targets both casual bloggers who want a simple out-of-the-box setup and more technical users or developers who need a customizable, self-hosted publishing framework with plugin and theme control.

features.title: Plugin architecture via Spartacus
features.description: A dedicated repository of community plugins and themes lets you extend core functionality without modifying Serendipity's codebase.

features.title: Multi-database support
features.description: Runs on MySQL, PostgreSQL, or SQLite, giving self-hosters flexibility in how they deploy the backend.

features.title: Rich entry editor
features.description: Includes CK Editor integration for writing and formatting blog entries in the admin dashboard.

features.title: Media database
features.description: Built-in media management for organizing images and files used in blog posts.

features.title: Smarty templating
features.description: Themes are built on the Smarty template engine, separating presentation from logic for easier customization.

features.title: Long-term security track record
features.description: The project emphasizes an active history of addressing security issues as one of its core project pillars.

pros: Mature project with over two decades of continuous development
Flexible plugin and theme system via Spartacus
Works with multiple database backends (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite)
Fully self-hosted, giving complete control over content and data
Free under the permissive BSD 3-Clause license
cons: Smaller plugin/theme ecosystem and community than mainstream platforms like WordPress
Requires basic PHP hosting knowledge to install and maintain
No built-in visual page-builder style editing

faqs.question: Is Serendipity free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the BSD 3-Clause license.

faqs.question: What databases does it support?
faqs.answer: MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQLite.

faqs.question: Is Serendipity still maintained?
faqs.answer: Yes, the project is on version 2.6 and remains actively developed on GitHub, with an active blog and forums.

faqs.question: How do I add features to my blog?
faqs.answer: Through the Spartacus plugin and theme repository, which houses community-contributed extensions.

use_cases.title: Personal blogging
use_cases.description: A lightweight, self-hosted platform for individuals who want full control over their blog without vendor lock-in.

use_cases.title: Customized publishing sites
use_cases.description: Developers can build tailored blog experiences using the plugin architecture and Smarty templates.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Serendipity (s9y) is a free, open-source PHP blogging platform with a plugin and theme ecosystem for self-hosted blogs.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source tool from La Digitale for creating and sharing curated collections of web links.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Digibunch is a free web application published by La Digitale, a French collective that builds non-commercial, open-source digital tools primarily for educators. It lets users create a "bouquet de liens" — a curated collection of web links, often used as a sitography or resource list built around a topic.

The application is released under the GNU AGPLv3 license with its source code hosted on Codeberg, and it sits alongside other La Digitale tools such as Digidrive as part of a broader suite of free, privacy-respecting web utilities.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Digibunch is aimed primarily at teachers and educators who need a simple way to compile and share a set of reference links with students, though its straightforward purpose makes it usable by anyone who wants to curate and share a themed list of URLs.

features.title: Curated link collections
features.description: Build a themed collection ("bouquet") of web links in one place, useful for sitographies and resource lists.

features.title: Free and open source
features.description: Released under the AGPLv3 license, with source code publicly available on Codeberg.

features.title: Part of the La Digitale suite
features.description: Works alongside other free, non-commercial educational tools built by the same collective.

features.title: No-cost, ad-free access
features.description: Follows La Digitale's model of offering tools free of charge without advertising or data monetization.

pros: Completely free with no ads or data monetization
Open source and self-hostable via the public Codeberg repository
Simple, purpose-built interface for a specific, common task
Backed by an established collective (La Digitale) with a track record of maintaining free edu-tech tools
cons: Narrow feature set focused on a single use case
Documentation and interface are primarily oriented toward French-speaking educators
Limited public information on the product beyond its source repository and tutorials

faqs.question: Is Digibunch free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's a free tool published by La Digitale under the AGPLv3 open-source license.

faqs.question: Who is Digibunch designed for?
faqs.answer: It's primarily built for teachers and educators, though anyone needing a simple way to share a list of links can use it.

faqs.question: Is the source code available?
faqs.answer: Yes, Digibunch's code is published on Codeberg under the AGPLv3 license.

use_cases.title: Classroom resource lists
use_cases.description: Teachers compile a sitography or set of reference links for a lesson or project.

use_cases.title: Themed link curation
use_cases.description: Anyone wanting to gather and share a focused list of web links around a topic.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Digibunch is a free, open-source tool from La Digitale for creating and sharing curated link collections, popular among teachers.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted bookmark manager with nested tags, instant search, and easy browser import.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Faved is an open-source bookmark manager (1.1K+ GitHub stars) built around a customizable nested-tag system for organizing large bookmark collections. It's designed to be lightweight and fast, with instant search across a user's entire library.

Faved can be self-hosted for free using a single Docker command, keeping all bookmark data under the user's own control, or used via a paid managed cloud offering for people who don't want to run their own server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Faved suits people managing large or growing bookmark libraries who want fast, structured organization, as well as self-hosting enthusiasts who want full data ownership and small teams needing a shared bookmark space.

features.title: Nested tagging system
features.description: Organize bookmarks with nested, color-coded tags that can be pinned for frequent use.

features.title: Instant search
features.description: Search across a bookmark library of any size with fast, one-click results.

features.title: Multi-platform capture
features.description: Save bookmarks via a Chrome extension, bookmarklet, or native share menus on mobile devices.

features.title: Duplicate detection
features.description: Automatically flags duplicate bookmarks to help keep a library clean.

features.title: Import from other tools
features.description: Import existing bookmarks from Chrome, Safari, Firefox, Edge, Raindrop, and Pocket while preserving structure.

features.title: Automatic metadata fetching
features.description: Captures and refreshes titles, descriptions, and preview images for saved links.

features.title: PWA support
features.description: Installable as a progressive web app with a responsive design across mobile, tablet, and desktop.

features.title: Bulk actions
features.description: Manage large sets of bookmarks at once with bulk editing and organizing tools.

pros: Self-hosted version is free forever with the full feature set
Lightweight and fast, with an easy one-command Docker setup
Flexible nested-tag organization system
Straightforward migration from Chrome, Safari, Firefox, Edge, Raindrop, and Pocket
Active open-source project with a growing GitHub community
cons: Cloud offering is newer and smaller than more established bookmarking services
Team/SSO functionality requires contacting sales for custom pricing
No dedicated native mobile apps beyond PWA and share-menu integration

faqs.question: Is Faved free?
faqs.answer: Self-hosting Faved is free forever. A managed cloud plan is also available starting at $2.50/month billed yearly, with a 14-day free trial.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Faved?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can be deployed with a single Docker command, giving you complete control over your data.

faqs.question: Can I import my existing bookmarks?
faqs.answer: Yes, Faved supports importing from Chrome, Safari, Firefox, Edge, Raindrop, and Pocket while preserving folder structure.

faqs.question: Is Faved open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, its codebase is public on GitHub with over 1,000 stars.

use_cases.title: Personal bookmark organization
use_cases.description: Keep large bookmark collections structured with nested tags and instant search.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted, privacy-first bookmarking
use_cases.description: Run Faved on your own server to keep full ownership of your saved links.

use_cases.title: Team bookmark sharing
use_cases.description: Use the Cloud Team plan for collaborative bookmark management with SSO.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Faved is an open-source, self-hosted bookmark manager with nested tags and fast search; free to self-host, with cloud plans from $2.50/month.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source identity backend behind Firefox Sync that advanced self-hosters can deploy independently of Mozilla.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: The Firefox Account Server, now generally referred to as Mozilla accounts (fxa), is the open-source identity and authentication system that powers Firefox Sync and other Mozilla-hosted services. Its codebase, maintained in the mozilla/fxa monorepo, consists of an auth-server that manages account data and authentication and a content-server that provides the web-based account management UI.

By default, Firefox points at Mozilla's own hosted instance at accounts.firefox.com, but Mozilla's own documentation describes how to run an independent instance by cloning the repository and deploying your own auth-server and content-server. Mozilla explicitly notes this self-hosting guide is preliminary and incomplete, and that self-hosters must also run their own Sync server (sync-1.5), since Mozilla's hosted sync servers will not trust account assertions issued by a third-party accounts server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: This is an advanced, infrastructure-level project intended for experienced sysadmins or organizations who want to run their own Firefox-Sync-compatible identity backend independent of Mozilla's hosted service — not something aimed at casual end users.

features.title: Auth-server + content-server architecture
features.description: Separates account/authentication logic from the web-based account management interface.

features.title: OAuth-based authentication
features.description: Provides the identity layer used by Firefox Sync and other Mozilla services.

features.title: Open-source Node.js codebase
features.description: Publicly maintained on GitHub as the mozilla/fxa monorepo.

features.title: Configurable client endpoint
features.description: Firefox can be pointed at a custom accounts server via browser configuration.

features.title: Documented (preliminary) self-hosting path
features.description: Mozilla provides instructions for cloning and deploying an independent instance, though the guide is explicitly marked as a work in progress.

pros: Free and open source, built from Mozilla's own production codebase
Enables full independence from Mozilla-hosted identity infrastructure
Actively used and maintained as Mozilla's real accounts system
cons: Mozilla's own self-hosting documentation is explicitly preliminary and incomplete
Requires also deploying your own Sync server for full functionality
Non-trivial deployment involving multiple Node.js services
Mozilla trademark restrictions apply to the content-server's branding for non-personal use

faqs.question: Can I self-host the Firefox/Mozilla accounts server?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mozilla's own documentation describes cloning the mozilla/fxa repository and deploying your own auth-server and content-server instances.

faqs.question: Is anything else required besides the accounts server?
faqs.answer: Yes — self-hosters must also run their own Sync server (sync-1.5), since Mozilla-hosted sync servers won't trust accounts issued by a third-party server.

faqs.question: Is this the same thing as Firefox Sync?
faqs.answer: No. It's the identity/account layer that Firefox Sync and other Mozilla services authenticate against.

faqs.question: Is the project actively maintained?
faqs.answer: Yes, the mozilla/fxa monorepo is Mozilla's production codebase for what is now branded Mozilla accounts.

use_cases.title: Independent Firefox-Sync-compatible identity backend
use_cases.description: Run your own accounts and sync infrastructure fully separate from Mozilla's hosted services.

use_cases.title: Reference architecture
use_cases.description: Study a real-world, production OAuth-based account server implementation.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: The Firefox/Mozilla accounts server (fxa) is Mozilla's open-source identity backend for Firefox Sync, self-hostable by advanced users.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hostable bookmark, note, and image manager with AI-powered auto-tagging and full-text search.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Karakeep (formerly Hoarder, launched in January 2023 and rebranded to Karakeep in 2025) is a self-hostable "bookmark everything" app for saving links, notes, images, and PDFs. It uses AI to automatically tag saved content for faster retrieval, and it's fully open source under AGPL-3.0 with 26k+ GitHub stars and 150+ contributors, developed by Localhost Labs Ltd.

Beyond bookmarking, Karakeep supports RSS feed subscriptions, automation rules, highlighting and organizing text from saved pages, and collaborative shared collections, all searchable through a comprehensive full-text search.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Karakeep is built for people who save large volumes of links, notes, and images and want automatic organization rather than manual tagging — including self-hosters looking for an open alternative to services like Pocket or Raindrop.

features.title: AI-powered auto-tagging
features.description: Automatically tags saved links, notes, and images using AI to speed up later retrieval.

features.title: Full-text search
features.description: Search across all saved content, including highlighted text from saved pages.

features.title: RSS feed subscriptions
features.description: Subscribe to feeds so new content is automatically collected into Karakeep.

features.title: Automation rules
features.description: Set up rules to automatically manage and organize incoming bookmarks.

features.title: Collaborative collections
features.description: Share and collaborate on bookmark collections with others.

features.title: REST API and webhooks
features.description: Integrate Karakeep with other tools and services via its API and webhook support.

features.title: Broad platform coverage
features.description: Available as apps for iOS, Android, Chrome, Firefox, and Safari, plus a web app and CLI tool.

pros: Large, active open-source community (26k+ GitHub stars, 150+ contributors)
AI tagging reduces manual bookmark organization work
Wide platform coverage across mobile, browser extensions, CLI, and API
Self-hosted version is free and fully featured
cons: AI tagging typically requires configuring an external AI provider, which can add cost
Cloud free tier is very limited (10 bookmarks, 20MB storage)
Self-hosting requires Docker and basic server management skills

faqs.question: Is Karakeep free?
faqs.answer: Self-hosting is free forever and fully featured. Cloud hosting has a limited free tier plus a Pro plan at $4/month ($40/year).

faqs.question: Was Karakeep previously called something else?
faqs.answer: Yes, it launched as Hoarder in January 2023 and was rebranded to Karakeep in 2025.

faqs.question: Does Karakeep have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, it offers a REST API with webhook support for integrations and automation.

faqs.question: What license is Karakeep under?
faqs.answer: AGPL-3.0.

use_cases.title: Personal knowledge management
use_cases.description: Centralize links, notes, and images with automatic AI tagging for fast retrieval.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted read-it-later alternative
use_cases.description: Replace services like Pocket or Raindrop with a self-hosted, open-source option.

use_cases.title: Team bookmark collaboration
use_cases.description: Use shared collections and the Corporate plan for organizational bookmark management.

features: AI tagging with your own AI provider

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Karakeep (formerly Hoarder) is an open-source, self-hostable bookmark, note, and image manager with AI auto-tagging and full-text search.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted bookmark manager that preserves full-page snapshots with AI tagging and team collaboration.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Linkwarden is an open-source bookmark manager (18k+ GitHub stars) that goes beyond simple link-saving by capturing full-page archives of saved content, helping guard against link rot. It's licensed under AGPL-3.0, with the entire source code published on GitHub, and can be self-hosted via Docker.

In addition to archiving, Linkwarden offers a reader view with annotation and highlighting, AI-powered automatic tagging, RSS feed integration inside collections, and sharing/collaboration for teams and public collections. Native iOS and Android apps and browser extensions are also available.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Linkwarden suits individuals and teams who want to permanently preserve web content — not just bookmark a URL — with searchable, annotated archives, and who value self-hosting for data ownership.

features.title: Full-page preservation
features.description: Archives full pages in formats like HTML to protect against content disappearing or link rot.

features.title: Reader view with annotation
features.description: Read saved pages with customizable fonts, highlighting, and annotation tools.

features.title: AI-powered tagging
features.description: Automatically organizes bookmarks using AI-driven tagging and smart search filters.

features.title: Team and public collections
features.description: Share collections privately with a team or publicly with anyone.

features.title: RSS feed integration
features.description: Subscribe to RSS feeds directly within a collection.

features.title: Native mobile apps
features.description: Dedicated iOS and Android apps in addition to a browser extension and installable PWA.

features.title: Bookmark sync and import/export
features.description: Sync bookmarks via Floccus and import or export existing bookmark data.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Secure API with access tokens for custom integrations and automation.

pros: Full-page archival goes beyond simple bookmarking to reduce link-rot risk
Large, active open-source community (18k+ GitHub stars)
Native iOS and Android apps, not just a PWA
AI-powered tagging built in
Self-hosted deployment unlocks all features for free
cons: Cloud plan is billed per user, which can add up for larger teams
Full-page archiving increases storage requirements compared to lightweight bookmarking tools
AI tagging may depend on configuring an external AI provider

faqs.question: Is Linkwarden free?
faqs.answer: Self-hosting is free and open source (AGPL-3.0) with all features unlocked. Cloud hosting starts at $3/month per user with a 14-day free trial.

faqs.question: Does Linkwarden save the full page, not just the link?
faqs.answer: Yes, it archives full pages (e.g., as HTML) to preserve content even if the original page later disappears.

faqs.question: Is there a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes, native iOS and Android apps are available in addition to a browser extension and PWA.

faqs.question: Does Linkwarden have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, a secure REST API with access tokens for custom integrations.

use_cases.title: Long-term research archival
use_cases.description: Preserve full copies of reference material so it remains accessible even if the source page changes or disappears.

use_cases.title: Team link collaboration
use_cases.description: Build and share collections of preserved, annotated pages with a team.

use_cases.title: Personal read-it-later with annotation
use_cases.description: Save, read, and highlight articles in a distraction-free reader view.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Linkwarden is an open-source, self-hosted bookmark manager that archives full web pages with AI tagging, annotation, and team collaboration.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted bookmark sync server and browser extension for syncing bookmarks without a cloud account.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: SyncMarks is an open-source bookmark synchronization system made up of two parts: a browser extension (available for Firefox, Edge, and Chrome/Chromium-based browsers) and a self-hosted PHP/JavaScript web app backend. Together they let users sync bookmarks across browsers and devices via their own server, communicating over a REST API and also supporting tab sync.

The project's philosophy is explicit about privacy: "your server, your data," with no cloud service, no account, and no tracking. It's licensed under GPLv3, with the extension distributed through Mozilla Add-ons and the Edge Store, and source code hosted on Codeberg and GitHub. The web app backend can also be used entirely on its own, as a standalone bookmark manager, without installing the extension.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: SyncMarks is aimed at privacy-focused users and self-hosters who want cross-browser bookmark sync without relying on a browser vendor's built-in cloud sync (such as Firefox Sync or Chrome Sync).

features.title: Cross-browser sync
features.description: Syncs bookmarks across Firefox, Microsoft Edge, and Chrome/Chromium-based browsers via a REST API.

features.title: Self-hosted backend
features.description: Runs entirely on your own PHP/JavaScript server, with no cloud account or third-party service involved.

features.title: Standalone web app
features.description: The backend can be used as a full bookmark manager on its own, without the browser extension.

features.title: Full bookmark management
features.description: Add, edit, move, and delete bookmarks from any connected device.

features.title: Tab sync
features.description: The browser extension also supports syncing open tabs alongside bookmarks.

features.title: Community translations
features.description: Interface translations are maintained through Weblate.

pros: Complete self-hosted control with no cloud dependency or tracking
Works across multiple browser vendors (Firefox, Edge, Chrome/Chromium)
Actively maintained project with regular releases
Usable as a standalone bookmark manager even without the extension
cons: Requires running your own PHP/database backend
Smaller community than mainstream browser-sync solutions
Setup requires some technical/self-hosting knowledge

faqs.question: Is SyncMarks free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the GPLv3 license.

faqs.question: Do I need the browser extension to use SyncMarks?
faqs.answer: No, the web app backend can be used standalone as a bookmark manager without installing the extension.

faqs.question: Which browsers does the extension support?
faqs.answer: Firefox, Microsoft Edge, and Chrome/Chromium-based browsers.

faqs.question: Where is my bookmark data stored?
faqs.answer: Entirely on your own self-hosted server — SyncMarks does not use any third-party cloud service.

use_cases.title: Cross-browser bookmark sync
use_cases.description: Keep bookmarks in sync across different browsers without relying on a vendor's cloud account.

use_cases.title: Standalone bookmark manager
use_cases.description: Use the self-hosted web app on its own as an alternative to browser-native bookmarking.
use_cases.audience: Individuals wanting full control of their bookmark data

enrichment.seo_meta_description: SyncMarks is a free, open-source, self-hosted bookmark sync server and browser extension for cross-browser syncing without a cloud account.

enrichment.short_description: Lightweight, self-hosted CalDAV and CardDAV server for privately syncing calendars and contacts across devices.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Baïkal is a lightweight, self-hosted CalDAV and CardDAV server for managing calendars and contacts across multiple devices while keeping data under the user's own control. It's built on the sabre/dav framework, was originally developed by Net Gusto and fruux, and now lives under the sabre.io project with ongoing community contributions.

Installation is minimal — Baïkal just needs a PHP-capable server plus either a MySQL or SQLite database — and it includes a web interface for managing users, address books, and calendars. It's released under the GNU GPLv3 license.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Baïkal is aimed at individuals, households, and small teams who want a private, self-hosted alternative to cloud calendar and contact syncing, and who already use CalDAV/CardDAV-capable apps like Apple Calendar/Contacts, Thunderbird, or DAVx5 on Android.

features.title: CalDAV and CardDAV protocol support
features.description: Syncs both calendars and contacts using the standard CalDAV/CardDAV protocols.

features.title: Web admin interface
features.description: Manage users, address books, and calendars through a built-in web UI.

features.title: Broad client compatibility
features.description: Works with iOS, macOS, Mozilla Thunderbird, DAVx5 on Android, and other CalDAV/CardDAV clients.

features.title: Minimal server requirements
features.description: Runs on any basic PHP-capable web server.

features.title: Flexible storage backends
features.description: Stores data in either MySQL or SQLite.

features.title: Built on sabre/dav
features.description: Uses the well-established sabre/dav library as its underlying protocol implementation.

pros: Very lightweight and quick to install, especially with SQLite
Broad compatibility with major calendar and contact clients
Minimal server requirements
Built on the mature, actively maintained sabre/dav framework
cons: Basic, utilitarian web interface with no built-in calendar view
No native mobile app — relies entirely on third-party CalDAV/CardDAV clients
Limited sharing/permission granularity compared to full groupware suites

faqs.question: Is Baïkal free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free open-source software licensed under GPLv3.

faqs.question: What database does Baïkal use?
faqs.answer: MySQL or SQLite.

faqs.question: What clients work with Baïkal?
faqs.answer: Any CalDAV/CardDAV client, including iOS, macOS, Thunderbird, and DAVx5 on Android.

faqs.question: Who maintains Baïkal?
faqs.answer: It's built on the sabre/dav framework, originally developed by Net Gusto and fruux, and now maintained under the sabre.io project with community contributions.

use_cases.title: Personal or family calendar and contacts sync
use_cases.description: Host your own private CalDAV/CardDAV server instead of relying on a third-party cloud provider.

use_cases.title: Small team calendar sharing
use_cases.description: Manage multiple users and shared calendars/address books from a single lightweight server.

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused alternative to cloud calendars
use_cases.description: Replace Google Calendar/Contacts sync with a self-hosted option.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Baïkal is a free, open-source, self-hosted CalDAV/CardDAV server for syncing calendars and contacts, compatible with iOS, macOS, and DAVx5.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted CalDAV server for sharing and synchronizing calendars, contacts, and free/busy scheduling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: DAViCal is a free, self-hosted CalDAV server for storing and synchronizing calendar data in iCalendar format across multiple users and clients. It was originally created by Andrew McMillan and later moved to a community support model in 2014.

It supports delegated read/write access so multiple users or clients can read and write the same calendar entries over time, along with free/busy time display for meeting scheduling and WebDAV access in either read-only or read-write mode. It runs on any PHP-capable web server backed by PostgreSQL, and works with clients including Mozilla Lightning, Evolution, Apple iCal, and iOS. It's released under the GPL.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: DAViCal suits small businesses, teams, and individual self-hosters who want shared calendaring with delegation and meeting-scheduling features, without adopting a full groupware or Exchange-style server.

features.title: CalDAV protocol server
features.description: Standards-based calendar sync compatible with a wide range of desktop and mobile clients.

features.title: Delegated access
features.description: Supports read/write delegation so multiple users can share and edit the same calendars.

features.title: Free/busy scheduling
features.description: Displays free/busy time to support scheduling meetings across users.

features.title: WebDAV access modes
features.description: Offers both read-only and read-write WebDAV access.

features.title: Broad client compatibility
features.description: Works with Mozilla Lightning, Evolution, Apple iCal, iOS, and other CalDAV clients.

features.title: PostgreSQL-backed storage
features.description: Uses PostgreSQL as its data store for calendar information.

pros: Mature project with a long-running, community-supported history
Strong multi-user delegation and calendar-sharing model
Works with major desktop and mobile CalDAV clients
Free and open source under the GPL
cons: Documentation and codebase reflect an older PHP 5-era design
Requires PostgreSQL specifically, less flexible than SQLite-based alternatives
The project itself does not recommend running it on Windows

faqs.question: Is DAViCal free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free software licensed under the GPL.

faqs.question: What database does DAViCal require?
faqs.answer: PostgreSQL.

faqs.question: What calendar clients work with DAViCal?
faqs.answer: Mozilla Lightning, Evolution, Apple iCal, iOS, and other CalDAV-capable clients.

faqs.question: Who created DAViCal?
faqs.answer: Andrew McMillan originally created it; the project moved to a community support model in 2014.

use_cases.title: Shared team calendaring
use_cases.description: Delegate calendar read/write access across a team or small office.

use_cases.title: Meeting scheduling with free/busy lookup
use_cases.description: Coordinate meetings using free/busy time display without a full Exchange-style server.

use_cases.title: Personal calendar hosting
use_cases.description: Run a private CalDAV server for individual use.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: DAViCal is a free, open-source, self-hosted CalDAV server for shared calendar sync, delegation, and free/busy meeting scheduling.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source calendar sync tool that consolidates events across Google Calendar, Outlook, iCloud, and CalDAV.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Keeper.sh is a calendar synchronization tool that aggregates events across personal, work, business, and school calendars and keeps them automatically updated. It supports Google Calendar, Outlook, iCloud, CalDAV, and generic ICS/iCal feeds, with customizable rules controlling which event details are shared between calendars.

It's open source under the AGPL-3.0 license and can be self-hosted via Docker images or manual deployment, or used as a hosted service. A conflict-reconciliation engine prevents orphaned or duplicate events, and it can also generate an aggregated, filterable iCal feed.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Keeper.sh serves both casual users juggling personal, work, and school calendars, and power users who need frequent sync intervals, unlimited linked accounts, and API access for automation.

features.title: Multi-provider sync
features.description: Syncs events across Google Calendar, Outlook, iCloud, CalDAV, and generic ICS/iCal feeds.

features.title: Customizable sync rules
features.description: Controls which event details are shared between linked calendars.

features.title: Conflict reconciliation
features.description: A sync engine that reconciles conflicting changes and prevents orphaned duplicate events.

features.title: Aggregated iCal feed
features.description: Generates a combined, filterable iCal feed from multiple source calendars.

features.title: Self-hostable
features.description: Deployable via Docker or manual installation using the open-source GitHub repository.

features.title: API access
features.description: Available on the Pro plan for programmatic access and automation.

pros: Open source (AGPL-3.0) with a genuine self-hosting option
Simple setup with no complex configuration required
Supports all major calendar providers plus generic CalDAV/ICS feeds
Affordable Pro tier for users needing frequent sync and API access
cons: Free tier is limited to 2 linked accounts and a 30-minute sync interval
Faster 1-minute sync and API access require the paid Pro plan
Newer project with a smaller community than established calendar tools

faqs.question: Is Keeper.sh free?
faqs.answer: There's a free tier with 2 linked accounts and 30-minute sync; the Pro plan is $5/month for unlimited accounts and 1-minute sync.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Keeper.sh?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's open source under AGPL-3.0 and can be self-hosted via Docker or manual deployment.

faqs.question: Which calendar providers are supported?
faqs.answer: Google Calendar, Outlook, iCloud, CalDAV, and ICS/iCal feeds.

faqs.question: Does Keeper.sh have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, API access is included with the Pro plan.

use_cases.title: Merging personal and work calendars
use_cases.description: Keep events across multiple accounts in sync without manually copying entries.

use_cases.title: Cross-platform calendar consolidation
use_cases.description: Combine calendars from different providers into one view without a full groupware suite.

use_cases.title: Automated calendar workflows
use_cases.description: Use the API to build custom automation on top of synced calendar data.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Keeper.sh is an open-source calendar sync tool that merges Google Calendar, Outlook, iCloud, and CalDAV into one view; free tier available.
